content.problemStatement:
**This is the hard version of the problem available for 100 points in all divisions. Note that here you need to find the minimum number of items possible. Further, you need to solve the problem for all prefixes of the input.**

There's a shop with NNN distinct items numbered 111, 222, .... NNN.
There's also a boolean array BBB of length NNN.

You want to buy all NNN items once each, in some order. Say this order is P1,P2,...,PNP_1, P_2, ..., P_NP1‚Äã,P2‚Äã,...,PN‚Äã. When buying item PiP_iPi‚Äã,



If BPi=1B_{P_i} = 1BPi‚Äã‚Äã=1 and there exists some j<ij < ij<i such that Pj>PiP_j > P_iPj‚Äã>Pi‚Äã, you get two copies of item PiP_iPi‚Äã, otherwise you get one copy. Informally, you get two copies if you have bought an item with a larger index before, otherwise you get one copy.




If BPi=0B_{P_i} = 0BPi‚Äã‚Äã=0, it's the opposite - if there exists some j<ij < ij<i such that Pj<PiP_j < P_iPj‚Äã<Pi‚Äã, you get two copies, otherwise one. Informally, you get two copies if you have bought an item with a smaller index before, otherwise you get one copy.



Let f(B)f(B)f(B) denote the  **minimum** total number of items that you can have in the end.

Given an array BBB of length NNN, find the fff value of all prefixes. Formally, find the NNN integers - f(B[1,1])f(B[1, 1])f(B[1,1]), f(B[1,2])f(B[1, 2])f(B[1,2]), f(B[1,3])f(B[1, 3])f(B[1,3]), ..., f(B[1,N])f(B[1, N])f(B[1,N]).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains NNN - the number of items
The second line contains NNN boolean variables - B1,B2,...,BNB_1, B_2, ..., B_NB1‚Äã,B2‚Äã,...,BN‚Äã.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN integers - f(B[1,1])f(B[1, 1])f(B[1,1]), f(B[1,2])f(B[1, 2])f(B[1,2]), f(B[1,3])f(B[1, 3])f(B[1,3]), ..., f(B[1,N])f(B[1, N])f(B[1,N])
constraints: The sum of NNN does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : No matter what order you choose to buy the items in, we will always end up with the answers 111 and 222.
Test Case 2 : For the entire array, the answer is 222 because we can choose the order P=[1,2]P = [1, 2]P=[1,2] which will only give us 111 copy of each.
